Walk-Forward Permutation Test¶

XGBoost on the S&P 500 — parallel Monte Carlo significance via bar permutation¶

Course: Simulacion Author: Jeronimo Deli

1. Why this test exists¶

Backtesting a quantitative strategy on a single historical path is an act of faith. With enough features, hyperparameters, or human pattern-matching, any model can produce a flattering equity curve in-sample. The danger is well known: a backtest measures the joint outcome of a real edge (if any) and the analyst's degrees of freedom.

The Walk-Forward Permutation Test (WFPT), introduced by David Aronson in Evidence-Based Technical Analysis (Wiley, 2007), confronts this directly. Its question is sharp:

Could the model's out-of-sample performance have arisen from price paths in which any predictable structure had been destroyed, while keeping the marginal distribution of bars intact?

Operationally: we permute the OHLC bars over the out-of-sample window (à la Aronson), re-run the entire walk-forward on each permuted path, and build the empirical null distribution of our test statistic. The $p$-value is the fraction of permuted runs at least as good as the real one.

Because the inner loop refits the model per permutation, the cost is roughly $n_{\text{perm}} \times$ a full backtest. The reference implementation in the repository runs $n=100$ permutations sequentially — too slow for serious work. This notebook scales to $n=1000$ permutations using joblib, with a clean modular wfpt/ package separating data, permutation, walk-forward, and reporting.

2. Setup¶

The reusable code lives in wfpt/. The notebook is a thin narrative wrapper.

In [1]:
import sys
from pathlib import Path

# make the wfpt/ package importable when running from notebooks/
ROOT = Path.cwd().parent if Path.cwd().name == "notebooks" else Path.cwd()
sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from wfpt import (
    load_sp500, make_features,
    permute_bars,
    walkforward_oos,
    sharpe_annualized, profit_factor, cagr,
    run_wfpt,
    plotting,
)

plotting.use_academic_style()
RNG_SEED = 42

print(f"numpy   {np.__version__}")
print(f"pandas  {pd.__version__}")
import xgboost, joblib, sklearn
print(f"xgboost {xgboost.__version__}   sklearn {sklearn.__version__}   joblib {joblib.__version__}")

numpy   2.2.6
pandas  2.3.2
xgboost 3.1.2   sklearn 1.7.2   joblib 1.5.2

3. Data — S&P 500 daily bars (2005–2024)¶

Twenty years of daily OHLCV from Yahoo Finance. Long enough to span multiple regimes (2008 GFC, 2010s low-vol bull, 2020 COVID, 2022 hike cycle), short enough to fit comfortably on a laptop.

In [2]:
df = load_sp500(start="2005-01-01", end="2024-12-31")
print(f"{len(df):,} bars  |  {df.index[0].date()}  ->  {df.index[-1].date()}")
df.head()

5,032 bars  |  2005-01-03  ->  2024-12-30
Out[2]:
Price Open High Low Close Volume
date
2005-01-03 1211.920044 1217.800049 1200.319946 1202.079956 1510800000
2005-01-04 1202.079956 1205.839966 1185.390015 1188.050049 1721000000
2005-01-05 1188.050049 1192.729980 1183.719971 1183.739990 1738900000
2005-01-06 1183.739990 1191.630005 1183.270020 1187.890015 1569100000
2005-01-07 1187.890015 1192.199951 1182.160034 1186.189941 1477900000

3.1 Quick look at the price path¶

In [3]:
log_close = np.log(df["Close"])
log_ret   = log_close.diff().dropna()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(9, 5.4), sharex=True)
axes[0].plot(log_close.index, log_close, color=plotting.PALETTE["null"], linewidth=1.0)
axes[0].set_ylabel("log Close")
axes[0].set_title("S&P 500 — log price")

axes[1].plot(log_ret.index, log_ret, color=plotting.PALETTE["muted"], linewidth=0.5)
axes[1].set_ylabel("daily log-return")
axes[1].axhline(0, color=plotting.PALETTE["ink"], linewidth=0.4)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"annualized mean   {log_ret.mean()*252:+.4f}")
print(f"annualized vol    {log_ret.std()*np.sqrt(252):.4f}")
print(f"skew              {log_ret.skew():+.3f}")
print(f"excess kurtosis   {log_ret.kurt():+.3f}")

annualized mean   +0.0797
annualized vol    0.1922
skew              -0.532
excess kurtosis   +13.040

4. Methodology¶

4.1 The null hypothesis — Aronson bar permutation¶

Each daily bar can be decomposed in log-space into four additive, semi-independent components:

$$ \begin{aligned} \text{gap}_t &= \log O_t - \log C_{t-1} \\ \text{body}_t &= \log C_t - \log O_t \\ \text{high}_t &= \log H_t - \log\max(O_t, C_t) \quad (\geq 0)\\ \text{low}_t &= \log\min(O_t, C_t) - \log L_t \quad (\geq 0) \end{aligned} $$

Under the null "no exploitable structure", these four series are exchangeable across time within the OOS window. The Aronson permutation independently shuffles each of the four component vectors and reconstructs OHLC from the last untouched close. The result:

  • Marginal distributions are preserved (volatility, gap distribution, candlestick shape).
  • Serial dependence is destroyed (no momentum, no mean-reversion, no regime persistence).

A model that genuinely captures temporal structure should produce a Sharpe higher than what these acausal paths can deliver.

4.2 The test statistic¶

$$T = \text{Sharpe}_{\text{annualized}}^{\text{OOS}} = \sqrt{252}\,\frac{\bar{r}_{\text{OOS}}}{s_{\text{OOS}}}$$

We compare the realized $T_{\text{real}}$ against the empirical distribution $\{T_{\text{perm}}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{n}$. Using the standard $+1$ correction:

$$\hat{p} = \frac{1 + \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{1}\{T_{\text{perm}}^{(i)} \geq T_{\text{real}}\}}{1+n}$$

4.3 The walk-forward — anchored¶

At each step $t$ we refit on $[0, t)$ and predict on $[t, t+\Delta)$. The training window grows with time — the anchored scheme — which mirrors how a desk would actually retrain in production. We use a 4-year initial train and a 21-day step (one trading month) between refits.

In [4]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.4))
plotting.plot_walkforward_schema(n_total=14, train_window=4, step=2, ax=ax)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4.4 What does a permutation actually look like?¶

To make the null tangible: we take the real S&P 500 path, fix the first 4 years of bars, and permute everything afterwards with a single seed. The bar shapes (volatility, gaps, wick structure) are statistically indistinguishable from the originals — only the sequence changes.

In [5]:
first_oos_idx = int(252 * 4)
df_perm_demo = permute_bars(df, start_idx=first_oos_idx, seed=7)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 3.8))
plotting.plot_permutation_example(df, df_perm_demo, start_idx=first_oos_idx, ax=ax)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5. The real strategy — baseline¶

We use a tightly-constrained XGBoost classifier (4 leaves deep, 120 trees, fixed hyperparameters — no tuning per fold to avoid inflating type-I error). Features: lagged log-returns 1..5, two volatilities, RSI(14), and a short/long MA ratio.

The signal is $\pm 1$ (long/short) and the strategy log-return is $s_t \cdot r_{t+1}$.

In [6]:
X_real, y_real, r_real = make_features(df)
TRAIN_WINDOW = 252 * 4
STEP         = 21

print(f"feature rows         {len(X_real):,}")
print(f"first OOS bar        {X_real.index[TRAIN_WINDOW].date()}")
print(f"OOS rows estimated   {len(X_real) - TRAIN_WINDOW:,}")

feature rows         5,010
first OOS bar        2009-02-04
OOS rows estimated   4,002
In [7]:
%%time
oos_real = walkforward_oos(
    X_real, y_real, r_real,
    train_window=TRAIN_WINDOW, step=STEP,
)
real_sharpe = sharpe_annualized(oos_real)
real_pf     = profit_factor(oos_real)
real_cagr   = cagr(oos_real)

print(f"OOS bars produced  {len(oos_real):,}")
print(f"Sharpe (annualized){real_sharpe:+.3f}")
print(f"Profit factor      {real_pf:.3f}")
print(f"CAGR               {real_cagr*100:+.2f}%")

OOS bars produced  4,002
Sharpe (annualized)+0.363
Profit factor      1.071
CAGR               +6.71%
CPU times: user 4.82 s, sys: 264 ms, total: 5.08 s
Wall time: 4.96 s

6. Monte Carlo permutation test — parallel¶

Set N_PERMUTATIONS below. The reference notebook runs 100 sequentially; we'll run 1000 in parallel across all CPU cores. Reduce to 200 if you only have a few minutes.

Each worker:

  1. Permutes OHLC from the train boundary onward (Aronson).
  2. Recomputes features on the permuted prices.
  3. Runs the entire anchored walk-forward.
  4. Returns the Sharpe and OOS return series.

Reproducibility across n_jobs settings is guaranteed via spawned SeedSequence — verified in tests/sanity.py.

In [10]:
N_PERMUTATIONS = 1000   # set to 200 for a quick run
N_JOBS         = 8    # all cores
In [11]:
result = run_wfpt(
    df,
    n_permutations=N_PERMUTATIONS,
    train_window=TRAIN_WINDOW,
    step=STEP,
    n_jobs=N_JOBS,
    master_seed=RNG_SEED,
    progress=True,
)

print(f"\nElapsed: {result.elapsed_seconds:.1f}s "
      f"({result.elapsed_seconds/N_PERMUTATIONS*1000:.0f} ms / permutation)")

WFPT (n_jobs=8):   0%|          | 0/1000 [00:00<?, ?it/s]
Elapsed: 708.7s (709 ms / permutation)

7. Results¶

In [12]:
summary = result.summary()
summary
Out[12]:
value
real_sharpe 0.362549
null_mean 0.369321
null_std 0.205751
null_q05 0.033122
null_q95 0.698219
n_perm_geq_real 508.000000
p_value 0.508492
elapsed_seconds 708.720083

7.1 Null distribution of the Sharpe ratio¶

In [13]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.4))
plotting.plot_null_distribution(
    real_value=result.real_sharpe,
    perm_values=result.perm_sharpes,
    p_value=result.p_value,
    metric_name="Sharpe ratio (annualized)",
    bins=40,
    ax=ax,
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

7.2 Equity curve vs permutation envelope¶

The shaded band is the [5th, 95th] percentile envelope of the permutation equity curves at each point in time. The dashed line is the permutation median.

If the real curve lives mostly inside the band — the strategy is indistinguishable from luck. If it consistently sits at or above the upper edge — there is signal.

In [14]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.8))
plotting.plot_equity_bands(
    real_returns=result.real_oos_returns,
    perm_returns=result.perm_oos_returns,
    quantiles=(0.05, 0.95),
    ax=ax,
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [17]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.0))

# all permuted equity curves — thin, low alpha so 1000 lines stay readable
for perm_ret in result.perm_oos_returns:
    eq = perm_ret.reindex(result.real_oos_returns.index).cumsum()
    ax.plot(eq.index, eq, color=plotting.PALETTE["null"],
            linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.06)

# real strategy on top
real_eq = result.real_oos_returns.cumsum()
ax.plot(real_eq.index, real_eq, color=plotting.PALETTE["real"],
        linewidth=1.8, label="Real strategy", zorder=10)

ax.axhline(0, color=plotting.PALETTE["muted"], linewidth=0.6)
ax.set_ylabel("Cumulative log-return")
ax.set_title(f"OOS equity curves — real vs all {len(result.perm_oos_returns)} permutations")
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

7.3 Bootstrap CI on the $p$-value¶

The $p$-value is itself a noisy statistic estimated from $n$ permutations. A 95% bootstrap CI tells us how much of its uncertainty is just Monte Carlo noise.

In [15]:
def bootstrap_p_ci(perm_sharpes, real_sharpe, n_boot=2000, alpha=0.05, seed=0):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    n = len(perm_sharpes)
    ps = np.empty(n_boot)
    for b in range(n_boot):
        idx = rng.integers(0, n, size=n)
        sample = perm_sharpes[idx]
        ps[b] = (1 + (sample >= real_sharpe).sum()) / (1 + n)
    return ps.mean(), np.quantile(ps, alpha/2), np.quantile(ps, 1 - alpha/2)

p_mean, p_lo, p_hi = bootstrap_p_ci(result.perm_sharpes, result.real_sharpe)
print(f"p-value (point) {result.p_value:.4f}")
print(f"p-value (boot)  {p_mean:.4f}   95% CI [{p_lo:.4f}, {p_hi:.4f}]")

p-value (point) 0.5085
p-value (boot)  0.5083   95% CI [0.4765, 0.5395]

8. Discussion¶

What the result means¶

Read the $p$-value strictly as: the probability of obtaining a Sharpe at least as high as ours, under a world where bars are exchangeable. It does not answer "is the strategy profitable in production" — it answers "is the model picking up real temporal structure or just noise".

Limitations of this test (worth being honest about)¶

  • Single test statistic. We chose Sharpe. A strategy with low Sharpe but a fat right tail could pass a profit-factor test and fail this one.
  • Same features and parameters per permutation. This isolates the signal extraction question. It does not control for the analyst's degrees of freedom in feature/parameter selection — that's a separate, broader test (e.g. White's reality check, Hansen's SPA).
  • Frictionless. No commissions, no slippage, no funding cost on shorts. A Sharpe of 1.0 here is not a Sharpe of 1.0 net.
  • Permutation destroys regime persistence. A model that exploits volatility clustering will look better than warranted, because the null also destroys clustering. Block-bootstrap would be the more conservative alternative.

What the parallelization buys¶

A sequential 1000-permutation run on this dataset takes roughly $n_{\\text{perm}} \\times t_{\\text{single WF}}$. With $\\sim$10 seconds per single WF, that's nearly three hours. Parallel across, say, 8 cores brings it well under half an hour, with bit-for-bit reproducible results because we spawn child seeds from a master SeedSequence rather than relying on the global RNG.

9. Reproducibility¶

  • Master seed: RNG_SEED = 42
  • Each permutation uses a child seed deterministically derived from the master.
  • n_jobs=-1 and n_jobs=1 give bit-identical results (verified in tests/sanity.py).
  • All XGBoost parameters fixed via wfpt.model.default_xgb_params().

To regenerate end-to-end: re-run all cells from the top in a clean kernel.
